result:
There is a moment—just before dawn, when it is neither night nor day—when the sky forgets to finish the sentence it began at sunset. I wait in the long parenthesis of that silence, shoes unlaced, coffee mercifully too hot to drink all at once, and I think about how strangely alive we become in the presence of unfinished stories. A half-written letter keeps breathing in its envelope; a porch light left on keeps believing someone is coming home; the cursor of an empty document pulses like an anxious, embryonic heart. The world is suddenly all apothecaries of potential: every leaf still folded in its bud, every sentence circling before it lands. I decide that if I must fill 2 500 words with whatever I want, the only honest way to begin is to start inside that blue gap between midnight’s last confession and the first finch note, and to admit I do not yet know where the thread ends.  

Perhaps it is better not to think of a thread at all, but rather a skein. Threads imply linearity—you pull, it unreels in one direction, it obliges with tidy forward motion. Skeins tangle, squirm, knot, loop back, kiss their own tails. Skeins insist on surfaces, volumes, a gallimaufry of colors South America once invented but later traded to Europe in exchange for gunpowder and smallpox. Here is a skein of mid-May in coastal Maine: lilacs still heady though the nights scrape frost across the windows, lobstermen swearing at buoys before school children have disagreed about breakfast, gulls pivoting on invisible thermals that smell like diesel and mussels. A skein holds the story of the alphabet as it leaked from Phoenician merchants to Greek scribes who added vowels like round windows in ship hulls, so ideas could breathe. I wind and unwind this yarn while my left hand keeps forgetting whether it is 2024 or has yawned backward into 1924, or 1824, when Maine was still part of Massachusetts and letters took months to stagger home from Canton by tall-masted clippers weighted down with porcelain, opium, and regret.  

I have set no timer save sunrise. When it arrives, countable as a coin flipped edge-over-edge, I will go inside and become diligent again. But while the sky lingers on the brink of verb tenses, I give myself permission to misremember, exaggerate, contradict. Sylvia Plath once said we should “live in constant preoccupation with immediate experience” and that sentence feels, against my tongue, like cool river stones pulled one by one from green water. Immediate experiences often arrive unlabeled, un-packaged by the customer service department. They spill: motors sputter, veins rupture, kisses ink themselves on the collarbone without consent forms. A storytelling mind walks into the freshly spilled moment like a child who happens upon an entire birthday party meant for someone else; nevertheless, the child snatches a pointed paper hat and helps himself to cake.  

Last week I and I drove eight hours north to hike a small mountain neither of us had ever read about on the internet. I say hike but really it was a negotiation with moss, a polite disagreement with spruce roots spread like careless tripwires, a conspiracy with chickadees who knew shortcuts. Somewhere just below treeline my legs declared mutiny. Half an hour later, muscles having re-sworn allegiance, we crested onto granite scraped clean by Pleistocene glaciers which, on that day, felt only the size of a teaspoon of regret. The summit performed its oldest magic trick: the treetops dropped away and the Atlantic walked toward us wearing a different salt perfume for every epoch it has crashed across basalt and sand. We ate mulberries and hard-boiled eggs, rationed water like mythic wanderers, talked about how all atoms heavier than hydrogen are essentially fossils of dead stars, and therefore we chew stellar ghosts at every breakfast. The wind was busy rearranging stratus clouds into loose braids while a red-tailed hawk boomeranged sunlight from its wings. At that altitude I realized how rarely we chance to see long horizons unaccompanied by snack bars, selfies, or the demeaning prerequisite of bucket lists. The mountain had no brand identity; it simply existed, which, nowadays, feels almost insurgent.  

I promised no direction, and already I hear an anxious reader wondering when the promised “point” will arrive. Yet most of life lacks plot arcs the way coral reefs lack outlines. Reefs grow sideways, simultaneously accreting and disintegrating, nudged by invisible chemistry and the genital whispering of moon cycles; they are not narratively economical. Perhaps humanity is simply one polyp among millions, persuaded by enclosure into the illusion that the rest of the reef exists to serve its story—rather than reveling in the unplotted ornamental excess. At this moment I am that polyp, nestled in my coralline body of paragraphs, offering secretions hardening into sentences that may someday, long after my carboniferous brief, become substrate for someone else’s unlikely reef.  

Have you ever had to learn your own fingerprints in the dark? I did, fumbling for a light switch in a childhood house still filled with the fossil-smell of lilacs from my mother’s perfume. One learns the terrain of ridges and whorls without intention the way a river leaf imprints its timing on clay. Decades later the finger that wore the whorl was the same finger that pressed against the cold hull of my father’s casket the day I discovered that death is never as metaphorical as poetry promised. Skin touches satin-lined wood. Skin also touches keypads, greased sandwich wrappers, scalps of brand new infants, infinite palm-sized screens that sell us back our loneliness with better resolution. Underneath the medallion of fingerprints the heart drums its monotonous syncopations, counting trips remaining: to primary care, to the barista who misspells even four-letter names, to cities waiting to uproot their tram tracks for nostalgia.  

When I rummage for an umbrella of unifying theme, what tumbles out instead is thick confetti: the perfumed bus ticket I once kept from Sarajevo to Belgrade because it smelled like rust and oranges; the tiny brass compass that belonged to my first girlfriend who left me for a woman she met at a laundromat shrine to Saint Jude; the way early ultraviolet mornings smell like copper pipes in old buildings; the way my aunt, holding my newborn cousin aloft, described the fontanelle as “the river entrance left open so dreams can come and go.” None of these items fit a single filing cabinet. Yet gathered together they begin to shimmer with siblinghood, the way dew on a grave marker does not explain grief but consoles it anyway.  

Last night I tried to describe sky to my four-year-old neighbor and failed. She wanted to know its color before God switched the night light on. She was dissatisfied with my refrain that the sky is not a color but an architecture of scattered light. She countered by insisting the sky is “tickle blue with puzzle edges,” and in that instant I forgave God for every job description theology has weighed him down with. A child’s metaphors are merciless. They arrive hand-in-glove, fully dressed, accepting no corrections at the wardrobe. I told her I would paint her a picture but by the time I returned with paper her mother had lured her inside with promises of bubble baths and lunar-spice cookies. I am left holding a watercolor set and the haunting suspicion that imagination migrates south at the exact age one starts caring about mortgage rates.  

Among the scattered items cluttering my desk is a black-and-white postcard of a fjord. Written on the back in my grandmother’s improbably looped cursive is a single line: “The place where time goes to learn patience.” Her handwriting has always reminded me of insects walking across paper in two rows—the commas and tail of a y like tiny segmented feet. She sent this from the deck of a cruise ship, age eighty-three, single after sixty-one years of marriage, sipping rye whisky because why not. She died before I could ask whether she’d glimpsed the whaleboats with red sails on scattered decks, or whether gulls had wheeled like gossip overhead. I keep the postcard propped against a jar of beach glass sorted by accidental gradations of pale bruise. Sometimes I turn it over, angle it under lamplight, but her cursive does not morph into supplementary confession no matter how long I gaze. The fjord merely stares back, a two-dimensional icicle.  

I should confess I am writing this from a house built in 1803, which means somewhere underneath the floorboards is a medallion of wood that inhaled candle smoke when Napoleon still raged across Europe unaware of his Waterloo sale rack destiny. Houses are slow-motion autobiographies: two hundred years of footsteps etch a silent rosary of wear patterns into the pine. I find babies’ heights penciled inside a closet so faint one could mistake them for insect trails. One resident added electricity; another crammed pink stereophonic panels into walls that once knew only whale oil. Someday someone will peel away the linoleum I tread on and find my dropped guitar pick among the strata; science will classify it as mid-2020s plastic symptomatic of a species obsessed with making disposable shapes that will outlast their makers.  

There appears to be a cardinal in the lilac bush again this spring. His plumage carries a red so saturated it feels narratively rude—like a semicolon blushed crimson. I learn that cardinals do not migrate. Instead they stay and transform cardiac arrest-level wind into the smallest possible unit of song. When I was twelve I believed such birds must be girls because “red” seemed inherently feminine; puberty taught me otherwise, as it taught me that the most trying dimensions of identity shift end-over-end, like shoes in a dryer, surprising toes inside. The cardinal delivers one metallic ting after another, as if operating a miniature blacksmith’s shop inside his throat. If wings are orchestrated longing, then feathers must be staff paper upon which the sky scribbles razor-thin bars. I have no proof of any of this, but I own the exhilaration.  

Among the myriad tabs currently open on my browser is an article about “micro-plastics inside human placentas,” another about a lab that resurrected the scent of extinct flowers from 100-year-old slides. The juxtaposition stings: we destroy and recreate simulae of beauty simultaneously, a parlor trick of late Anthropocene narcissism. I imagine a biotech start-up someday bottling the odor of vanished coral mating rituals, marketing it as a high-end fragrance called Eau de Collapse. The tagline: “Wear the memory of abundance.”  

But maybe cynicism is merely fireflies trapped in guilt jars. There is also the farmer in Maine who parks his pickup beside the blueberry barrens each June, stereo blasting Vivaldi because bees apparently pollinate more enthusiastically to the Four Seasons. He claims baroque strings massage pollen from anthers; perhaps krunk-rap would yield jam so nervous it refuses to set. I imagine the meadow drunk on violins, each bee a vibrating connoisseur of counterpoint. If this is placebo, it delights me anyway.  

Someone—let’s call him Eliot, after the poet, or after the teenager who gave me my first mixtape—told me once that every language invents a shortcut between nostalgia and mathematics. In Japanese, mono no aware: the bittersweet sharpened edge of ephemerality. In Portuguese, saudade: the longing that outlives the presence. In Icelandic, sumarhnút—literally “summer knot,” the amalgam of joy and ache when sun dips below midnight horizon. Eliot argued any given human has a budget of translatable sadness; waste it on the wrong broken umbrella and you have none left for cancer diagnoses. I think of this as I rinse coffee grounds into the compost, hoping microbes will transubstantiate caffeine into tomatoes.  

Tonight in the kitchen I played the radio game: spin the dial until a voice emerges speaking a language you don’t know. You assign whatever emotional weather you need to the syllables. An announcer in Icelandic mournfully recounts—if my ears decide—how submarines dream of barnacles. Another station in Tajikistan exuberantly describes the migration of apricot pits through wormholes. None of this is accurate, but it is true how story floods the absence of certainty. I once ended up listening to an Irish-language program that sounded like a wet stone pressed softly against another stone: consonants clicked, vowels pooled and then vanished. I had just broken up with someone for the second time and the incomprehensible grief in the broadcaster’s tone matched my own so perfectly that I cried custard-colored tears onto the cutting board where I was chopping onions. The circles aligned.  

There is a kind of safety in words you do not understand, the same safety found in painting a bedroom midnight blue and deciding that ceiling is sky enough for now. My father used to kiss the top of my head when I skinned knees, saying the hurt was jumping from my body into his. He did not, could not, absorb the scrape; yet the utterance wrapped around the pain like duck tape around shattered greenhouse glass. I sometimes borrow that parenting phrase and whisper it to strangers I will never see again—cashiers administering wobbly wheelchairs, toddlers with skeptical eyes at grocery store height. “I take your sorrow for one heartbeat,” I say silently, like passing currency that instantly devalues but leaves behind oxidized promise. Perhaps that is the real meaning of prayer: not petitioning omnipotent CEOs for services, but enacting small, ridiculous barters of empathy between colliding particles.  

We crave endings so zealously that we invent them even when none are warranted. Consider how the human eye loves the horizon—which is, strictly speaking, the place where vision collapses into defeat. Still we stand on beaches in thalassic love affairs, posting photos captioned “forever,” while tides reset the hourglass every twelve job duties. The sheer nerve of physics! To reopen the epilogue again and again with recyclable salt applause.  

When my niece was seven she drew a picture of heaven populated exclusively by winged sloths drinking milkshakes through curly straws. I asked how sloths manage aerodynamics; she stated they flap slowly but have forever. At this point every adult in her vicinity exhaled as though one small gateway inside eternity clicked momentarily open, the way airplane windows reveal for a flash an ungoverned luminosity above the clouds. The drawing now hangs magnetized to my refrigerator among coupons and wedding invitations. Next to it is a to-do list I wrote in grayscale marker: pay the dentist, return library to books, apologize to the moon for not noticing Phase Five last night. The moon, in cursive italic, refuses to file complaints. The sloths don’t seem worried either. They stir their malts clockwise as galaxies do.  

A week ago I dreamed I attended my own funeral. Mid-service I interrupted the eulogist because she got my pronouns wrong and my favorite song wrong and she kept spitting when she spoke which made blackberries sprout from the floorboards and coil up the pews like time-lapse garden serpents. Everyone clapped politely. I woke, unsurprisingly, exhausted. Dream anatomy suggests funerals for the living are rehearsals, but perhaps they are simply projections of the hive mind trying on grief like costume jewelry. Either way, I’ve since practiced listening to others with a softer receiver, convinced that kindness is a thread-binding spell ripped prematurely from fairy tales before capitalism could franchise it into branded toiletries.  

Yesterday on the pier an old man sold bouquets of kelp tied with used lobster bands. He called them “seaweed prayers.” No one bought any, yet he smiled oceanic level-ups to every passerby. I asked what a seaweed prayer does. He replied it tides over your thirst for meaning with saltier questions. I bought one for a single dollar. The kelp smelled so vividly of low-tide decay that my nostrils achieved a sort of punk-rock transcendence—like tasting centuries before any nervous humans tried to blame each other for weather. I left the bouquet bobbing in the harbor to continue praying on its own; it will dissolve exactly on schedule, without ego agony.  

I keep circling back to the woman I loved in a city obsessed with omissions. We wrote one another letters in invisible ink: lemon juice at first, then white crayon on cardstock, finally simply typing emails whose words matched background color—select-all to read. The game of hiding expanded until we vanished from one another. Every time I run spell-check after midnight, the cursor jumps erratically, as though searching for paragraphs I refused to send. A deleted document never entirely vacates; it compresses into meta-data ghosts. Perhaps one day forensic software will decrypt our muted correspondence and archive the empty spaces. The archivists’ citation will read: “Wherein two people demonstrate affection via absence, the geography of deliberate longing.” I hope footnotes list the juiciness of lemons that July, to honor citrus.  

What else deserves homage before sunrise officially erupts? The sound of my neighbor’s old dog arranging arthritis across the porch. The toaster whose cord has been chewed by a rodent yet miraculously still works, exposing copper innards like guts at an anatomy theatre. The constellation of minute burn marks on my writing desk from when I used to chain-smoke clove cigarettes and believe the smoke curling into question marks was a type of argument with fate. The clove habit died but the scars remain, idiot constellations consoling nobody. I trace them like Braille where the story tries to tell me, again and again: you were here, you were reckless, you remain.  

Language begins, says linguist John McWhorter, as a workaround for how much our eyes can convey when they are unable to cry in unison. Right now my eyes fill with leftover galaxies because I can hear, through the open window, the town’s first snowplow. The blade scrapes asphalt. It is —astonishingly—May. Climate rupture has resorted to sensory gaslighting. Yet the whining plow reverberates like a didgeridoo played by someone who only believes in mechanical explanations. I imagine the driver inside, thermos perched on dashboard, sighing at glaciated salt reminders while a playlist leaks vintage Rihanna. We are godshatter performing janitorial service for ourselves—I write this without mockery but devotion so swollen I can bruise it with a fork.  

If you’ve borne with me this far, suppose we build an imaginary museum. Entry is free but you must circumnavigate the globe first, barefoot. Inside, artifacts label themselves in first-person:  
Hello, I am the elm leaf that insisted on growing heart-shaped despite inherited instruction sets;  
I am the exclamation mark the editor deleted because enthusiasm was deemed impolitic;  
I am the unlabeled tape cassette containing whale songs overdubbed with heartbeat of an unborn child from 1987;  
I am the fur stoles in your grandmother’s closet still awaiting the return of winter in July.  

There is a mirror at exit asking only one question: Which exhibit startled you into remembering you are unfinished? Visitors exit, allegedly, unchanged but humming leakage. Critics review the museum harshly: “Too sentimental, lacks metric.” I respond with the free-range twinge in my ribcage that fattens on attention. Art isn’t always about optics. Sometimes it is an exoskeleton for everything that has no market capitalization.  

Even this essay—if that is what this has become—lacks the decent humility of brevity. Yet the same sky that couldn’t finish its sentence at sunset still can’t; the faintest onion-skin layer of light pries open horizon like a love letter. Cardinal has been replaced by robin. The coffee is finally cool enough to gulp. Somewhere the fjord begins thawing, all those syllables of ice unfastening themselves from vowel to vowel. My grandmother’s handwriting floats up from postcard, now soundtrack, now report from far-flung limbic embassy. I read once that if you lean close enough to the lips of a sleeping person you can sometimes witness syllables forming before the sleeper does; dreams rehearse their exits privately.  

Now the robin decides to screech the world awake. The sound is the sound of something small insisting largeness exists. I think of my niece’s sloths milkshaking the stratosphere. I think of the kelp bouquet undissolved probably, continuing its salty rosary around pilings. I think of taxonomy itself as a love note wedged inside uncertainty—barnacles cling even to whale bellies to hitch a slow-motion diaspora.  

So let me close with not closure, but a caesura: an inhalation where the story steps aside to watch itself arrive again and again in waves. There is a palimpsest of horizons patterned inside our long bones, etched by migrating birds whose names we inherit accented differently every century. Whenever you feel the sentence snapping like ice too thin to hold, step out onto your own porch at the moment dawn forgets to finish. Breathe one syllable of the sky’s unsentence. Hold it like an unlabeled present. Know by its quiver that the gift is its own wrapping. The weight you feel is merely the cosmos gently anchoring itself to your eardrum so it can listen, through you, to the next improbable robin.

I sometimes borrow that parenting phrase and whisper it to strangers I will never see again—cashiers administering wobbly wheelchairs, toddlers with skeptical eyes at grocery store height. “I take your sorrow for one heartbeat,” I say silently, like passing currency that instantly devalues but leaves behind oxidized promise. Perhaps that is the real meaning of prayer: not petitioning omnipotent CEOs for services, but enacting small, ridiculous barters of empathy between colliding particles.  

We crave endings so zealously that we invent them even when none are warranted. Consider how the human eye loves the horizon—which is, strictly speaking, the place where vision collapses into defeat. Still we stand on beaches in thalassic love affairs, posting photos captioned “forever,” while tides reset the hourglass every twelve job duties. The sheer nerve of physics! To reopen the epilogue again and again with recyclable salt applause.  

When my niece was seven she drew a picture of heaven populated exclusively by winged sloths drinking milkshakes through curly straws. I asked how sloths manage aerodynamics; she stated they flap slowly but have forever. At this point every adult in her vicinity exhaled as though one small gateway inside eternity clicked momentarily open, the way airplane windows reveal for a flash an ungoverned luminosity above the clouds. The drawing now hangs magnetized to my refrigerator among coupons and wedding invitations. Next to it is a to-do list I wrote in grayscale marker: pay the dentist, return library to books, apologize to the moon for not noticing Phase Five last night. The moon, in cursive italic, refuses to file complaints. The sloths don’t seem worried either. They stir their malts clockwise as galaxies do.  

A week ago I dreamed I attended my own funeral. Mid-service I interrupted the eulogist because she got my pronouns wrong and my favorite song wrong and she kept spitting when she spoke which made blackberries sprout from the floorboards and coil up the pews like time-lapse garden serpents. Everyone clapped politely. I woke, unsurprisingly, exhausted. Dream anatomy suggests funerals for the living are rehearsals, but perhaps they are simply projections of the hive mind trying on grief like costume jewelry. Either way, I’ve since practiced listening to others with a softer receiver, convinced that kindness is a thread-binding spell ripped prematurely from fairy tales before capitalism could franchise it into branded toiletries.  

Yesterday on the pier an old man sold bouquets of kelp tied with used lobster bands. He called them “seaweed prayers.” No one bought any, yet he smiled oceanic level-ups to every passerby. I asked what a seaweed prayer does. He replied it tides over your thirst for meaning with saltier questions. I bought one for a single dollar. The kelp smelled so vividly of low-tide decay that my nostrils achieved a sort of punk-rock transcendence—like tasting centuries before any nervous humans tried to blame each other for weather. I left the bouquet bobbing in the harbor to continue praying on its own; it will dissolve exactly on schedule, without ego agony.  

I keep circling back to the woman I loved in a city obsessed with omissions. We wrote one another letters in invisible ink: lemon juice at first, then white crayon on cardstock, finally simply typing emails whose words matched background color—select-all to read. The game of hiding expanded until we vanished from one another. Every time I run spell-check after midnight, the cursor jumps erratically, as though searching for paragraphs I refused to send. A deleted document never entirely vacates; it compresses into meta-data ghosts. Perhaps one day forensic software will decrypt our muted correspondence and archive the empty spaces. The archivists’ citation will read: “Wherein two people demonstrate affection via absence, the geography of deliberate longing.” I hope footnotes list the juiciness of lemons that July, to honor citrus.  

What else deserves homage before sunrise officially erupts? The sound of my neighbor’s old dog arranging arthritis across the porch. The toaster whose cord has been chewed by a rodent yet miraculously still works, exposing copper innards like guts at an anatomy theatre. The constellation of minute burn marks on my writing desk from when I used to chain-smoke clove cigarettes and believe the smoke curling into question marks was a type of argument with fate. The clove habit died but the scars remain, idiot constellations consoling nobody. I trace them like Braille where the story tries to tell me, again and again: you were here, you were reckless, you remain.  

Language begins, says linguist John McWhorter, as a workaround for how much our eyes can convey when they are unable to cry in unison. Right now my eyes fill with leftover galaxies because I can hear, through the open window, the town’s first snowplow. The blade scrapes asphalt. It is —astonishingly—May. Climate rupture has resorted to sensory gaslighting. Yet the whining plow reverberates like a didgeridoo played by someone who only believes in mechanical explanations. I imagine the driver inside, thermos perched on dashboard, sighing at glaciated salt reminders while a playlist leaks vintage Rihanna. We are godshatter performing janitorial service for ourselves—I write this without mockery but devotion so swollen I can bruise it with a fork.  

If you’ve borne with me this far, suppose we build an imaginary museum. Entry is free but you must circumnavigate the globe first, barefoot. Inside, artifacts label themselves in first-person:  
Hello, I am the elm leaf that insisted on growing heart-shaped despite inherited instruction sets;  
I am the exclamation mark the editor deleted because enthusiasm was deemed impolitic;  
I am the unlabeled tape cassette containing whale songs overdubbed with heartbeat of an unborn child from 1987;  
I am the fur stoles in your grandmother’s closet still awaiting the return of winter in July.  

There is a mirror at exit asking only one question: Which exhibit startled you into remembering you are unfinished? Visitors exit, allegedly, unchanged but humming leakage. Critics review the museum harshly: “Too sentimental, lacks metric.” I respond with the free-range twinge in my ribcage that fattens on attention. Art isn’t always about optics. Sometimes it is an exoskeleton for everything that has no market capitalization.  

Even this essay—if that is what this has become—lacks the decent humility of brevity. Yet the same sky that couldn’t finish its sentence at sunset still can’t; the faintest onion-skin layer of light pries open horizon like a love letter. Cardinal has been replaced by robin. The coffee is finally cool enough to gulp. Somewhere the fjord begins thawing, all those syllables of ice unfastening themselves from vowel to vowel. My grandmother’s handwriting floats up from postcard, now soundtrack, now report from far-flung limbic embassy. I read once that if you lean close enough to the lips of a sleeping person you can sometimes witness syllables forming before the sleeper does; dreams rehearse their exits privately.  

Now the robin decides to screech the world awake. The sound is the sound of something small insisting largeness exists. I think of my niece’s sloths milkshaking the stratosphere. I think of the kelp bouquet undissolved probably, continuing its salty rosary around pilings. I think of taxonomy itself as a love note wedged inside uncertainty—barnacles cling even to whale bellies to hitch a slow-motion diaspora.  

So let me close with not closure, but a caesura: an inhalation where the story steps aside to watch itself arrive again and again in waves. There is a palimpsest of horizons patterned inside our long bones, etched by migrating birds whose names we inherit accented differently every century. Whenever you feel the sentence snapping like ice too thin to hold, step out onto your own porch at the moment dawn forgets to finish. Breathe one syllable of the sky’s unsentence. Hold it like an unlabeled present. Know by its quiver that the gift is its own wrapping. The weight you feel is merely the cosmos gently anchoring itself to your eardrum so it can listen, through you, to the next improbable robin.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
